tested_predictions.hypothesis: XGBoost will outperform logistic regression by ≥4pp in macro_f1 because Total_EMI_per_month (MI=0.5471) provides strong non-linear signal (F=1.04) that linear models miss, and there are no |r|>0.8 redundancies so boosting efficiently splits on the top MI features
tested_predictions.basis: Top MI concentration in Total_EMI_per_month (0.5471) with low ANOVA F (1.04) indicating non-linear separation + absence of high correlations

tested_predictions.hypothesis: Total_EMI_per_month will rank #1 in global SHAP importance with mean|SHAP| ≥1.5× the #2 feature because its mutual_information=0.5471 is the highest and its low F-statistic=1.04 signals non-linear relationships that tree models exploit
tested_predictions.basis: Total_EMI_per_month MI=0.5471 vs next-best MI=0.5187 and F=1.04

tested_predictions.hypothesis: Missingness indicator for Type_of_Loan will appear in the top-5 features in global SHAP because MNAR pattern shows missing_pct=11.41% with max_target_dist_diff=19.51pp
tested_predictions.basis: Type_of_Loan missing_pct=0.1141 correlates with target by 0.1951

supported_conjectures.hypothesis: Standard class will have the lowest recall (≤0.65) because its class-conditional means on Total_EMI_per_month (μ=1399) and Delay_from_due_date (μ=19.75) sit between Good and Poor, creating two decision boundaries and EMI’s weak linear separability (F=1.04) exacerbates confusion
supported_conjectures.basis: class_means overlap for Standard; EMI F=1.04; Delay_from_due_date F=11471 shows ordinal separation but EMI dominates MI
supported_conjectures.evidence_needed: Per-class recall and confusion matrix from evaluate-models showing Standard→Poor vs Standard→Good counts

supported_conjectures.hypothesis: Delay_from_due_date will rank 3rd in XGBoost SHAP importance with mean|SHAP| ~0.4–0.6× the top feature because its F-statistic=11471.89 indicates strong linear signal but MI=0.1215 shows limited non-linear gain
supported_conjectures.basis: ANOVA F=11471.89 vs MI=0.1215 for Delay_from_due_date
supported_conjectures.evidence_needed: Global SHAP ranking comparison at global-xai

supported_conjectures.hypothesis: Monthly_Inhand_Salary will occupy the #2 SHAP slot with mean|SHAP| ≥0.8× the top because its MI=0.5187 and F=1903.07 both rank it as the second-strongest linear and non-linear predictor
supported_conjectures.evidence_needed: SHAP feature importance distribution from global-xai

exploratory_leads.hypothesis: The ratio Total_EMI_per_month/Monthly_Inhand_Salary will yield higher mutual information (>0.60) than either feature alone because they each contribute independent signal (no |r|>0.8)
exploratory_leads.basis: No high correlations among top MI features + both MI>0.5
exploratory_leads.how_to_test: Engineer the ratio, compute MI and include in model to compare SHAP rank versus individual features

exploratory_leads.hypothesis: Interaction term Interest_Rate × Delay_from_due_date will capture high-risk customers with combined high values, raising MI above individual MI=0.1705 and MI=0.1215
exploratory_leads.basis: Interest_Rate MI=0.1705, Delay_from_due_date MI=0.1215, low pairwise correlation
exploratory_leads.how_to_test: Add the product feature, measure MI and assess feature importance in boosted tree

exploratory_leads.hypothesis: Adding a binary flag for missing Monthly_Inhand_Salary (15.0% missing) will boost model performance because salary has skew=1.1273 and missingness likely MNAR
exploratory_leads.how_to_test: Include missing indicator, retrain models, and compare macro_f1

eda_hypotheses.model_selection_prediction: XGBoost will achieve macro_f1 ≥0.75, outperforming logistic regression by ≥4pp, because high MI in the non-linearly separable Total_EMI_per_month and absence of redundant features favor boosted trees
eda_hypotheses.class_struggle_prediction: Standard will have the lowest recall (≤0.65) with Standard→Poor as the dominant confusion pair

0.diagnosis: Recall=0.7819. Of the 21.8% errors, 11.5% go to class 1 vs 10.3% to class 2, indicating that class 0 instances near the 0–1 boundary bleed slightly more into class 1. Feature overlap on medium EMI and moderate delays likely causes this.

1.diagnosis: Recall=0.5927, lowest of all classes. Errors split 21.6%→class 0 vs 19.1%→class 2, showing the class 1 boundary is porous on both sides but slightly more toward class 0. This suggests linear and non-linear features fail to carve out the middle class cleanly.

2.diagnosis: Recall=0.8452. Misclass rate 15.5% is concentrated: 12.2%→class 1 vs 3.3%→class 0, indicating most class 2 errors cross into class 1, likely where delay and EMI patterns overlap borderline good vs poor borrowers.

capacity_analysis.logistic_regression: Underfitting. Best C=0.243 indicates moderate L2 regularization suppressing coefficient magnitude. CV macro_f1=0.6423 vs test=0.6534 (1.1 pp gap) shows no overfitting but inability to capture non-linear interactions; 3.5 pp deficit to XGBoost confirms complex signal.
capacity_analysis.random_forest: Well-fitted. Best max_depth=10, min_samples_split=9. CV macro_f1=0.6760 vs test=0.6811 (0.5 pp gap) with no train-test divergence, indicating capacity matches data complexity and RF captures moderate interactions.
capacity_analysis.xgboost: Well-fitted. Best max_depth=6, η=0.0139 with subsample/colsample≈0.8. CV macro_f1=0.6733 vs test=0.6902 (1.0 pp gap) and near-identical train/validation loss trajectories show stable convergence without overfitting; slow learning rate trades speed for generalization.

confidence_analysis.summary: LogisticRegression exhibits a narrow correct–wrong gap (~0.026), signaling weak self-awareness. RandomForest widens the gap to ~0.076, and XGBoost to ~0.109, indicating XGBoost best knows when it’s correct. Across all models, class 1 has the lowest mean confidence on both correct and wrong predictions, reflecting its high confusion.

training_diagnostics.learning_curve_interpretation: XGBoost train and validation logloss decrease in lockstep over 1000 rounds, flattening after ~200 rounds with no divergence, indicating sufficient capacity, no overfitting, and potential for marginal gains with more rounds.

tested.original: XGBoost will outperform logistic regression by ≥4pp in macro_f1 because Total_EMI_per_month (MI=0.5471) provides strong non-linear signal ...
tested.actual: XGBoost macro_f1=0.6902 vs LR=0.6534, gap=3.68 pp (<4pp)

tested.original: Total_EMI_per_month will rank #1 in global SHAP importance with mean|SHAP| ≥1.5× the #2 feature ...
tested.actual: Global SHAP data not yet available for validation

tested.original: Missingness indicator for Type_of_Loan will appear in the top-5 features in global SHAP ...
tested.actual: Global SHAP data not yet available for validation

supported.original: Standard class will have the lowest recall (≤0.65) because its class-conditional means ...
supported.evidence: Observed recall for class 1=0.5927 (below 0.65) confirms Standard struggles most

tested_predictions.hypothesis: Applying a probability threshold of ≥0.70 on XGBoost predictions will raise class 1 precision to ≥0.75 while reducing recall by ≤20%.
tested_predictions.basis: XGBoost class 1 correct mean confidence=0.7784 vs wrong=0.6204 suggests a usable threshold.

tested_predictions.hypothesis: Including EMI_Salary_Ratio will reduce RF confusion flow 0→1 by ≥10%.
tested_predictions.basis: RF class 0→1 error=8.8% and interaction feature designed to separate EMI clusters.

supported_conjectures.hypothesis: RandomForest’s 3 pp recall boost on class 0 over LR indicates EMI_Salary_Ratio interaction drives better separation of class 0.
supported_conjectures.evidence_needed: Feature-level SHAP interaction effects for EMI_Salary_Ratio

exploratory_leads.hypothesis: Calibrating XGBoost probabilities via isotonic/regression calibration will widen the confidence gap by ≥5%.
exploratory_leads.basis: Current correct–wrong gap=0.109; calibration often sharpens probabilities.
exploratory_leads.how_to_test: Apply calibration on validation set and measure mean confidence gap

exploratory_leads.hypothesis: Stacking logistic regression on XGBoost leaves will improve macro_f1 by ≥0.5 pp.
exploratory_leads.basis: Complementary error patterns: LR catches some linear cases XGBoost misses.
exploratory_leads.how_to_test: Train a meta-model on XGBoost leaf indices and evaluate cross-validated macro_f1

exploratory_leads.hypothesis: Segmenting the dataset by EMI_Salary_Ratio quartiles and training separate tree models per segment will boost overall macro_f1 by ≥1 pp.
exploratory_leads.basis: Non-linearity across EMI_Salary_Ratio ranges suggests heterogeneous decision surfaces.
exploratory_leads.how_to_test: Divide data into quartiles by EMI_Salary_Ratio, train separate XGBoost models, and aggregate predictions

observations: Credit_Mix is the top SHAP feature (mean_abs_shap=0.4982) and also ranks #1 in grouped PFI (importance_mean=0.1591)
Outstanding_Debt is #2 by SHAP (0.3004) and #2 by grouped PFI (0.0608)
Delay_from_due_date ranks only #6 in SHAP (0.1051) but #3 in grouped PFI (0.0467)
Month is #3 in SHAP (0.2199) but drops to #10 in grouped PFI (0.0030)
Num_Credit_Card is #5 in SHAP (0.1244) yet only #11 in grouped PFI (0.0024)
PDP for Credit_Mix shows class2 probability rising from 0.0736 at value 0 to 0.4268 at value 2
insights: Delay_from_due_date’s high PFI but lower SHAP suggests it drives performance mainly via interactions attributed by SHAP to other features
Month’s high SHAP but low PFI implies its seasonal signal is context-specific and redundant when marginalized, so shuffling it has little marginal impact

feature_importance_consensus.interpretation: The core agreement on Credit_Mix, Outstanding_Debt and Interest_Rate indicates these features drive both marginal loss and individual contributions. SHAP-only features (Month, Num_Credit_Card) carry interaction-specific or seasonal signals that PFI undervalues, while PFI-only features (Delay_from_due_date, Credit_History_Age) contribute through interactions SHAP disperses to correlated proxies.

Credit_Mix.class_direction: Higher categories (2) sharply increase class2 probability while suppressing class1

Outstanding_Debt.class_direction: Very low and very high debt both boost class2 probability while mid-range debt favors class1

Month.class_direction: Months ≥3 reduce class1 probability and raise class2

Interest_Rate.class_direction: High interest rates (>15) markedly raise class0 probability and lower class1

Num_Credit_Card.class_direction: Increasing number of cards steadily raises class0 probability and lowers class2

cross_layer_validation.eda_to_xai: EDA predicted Total_EMI_per_month and Monthly_Inhand_Salary would dominate, but global XAI shows Credit_Mix and Outstanding_Debt instead; engineered EMI_Salary_Ratio (EDA MI>0.60 expected) ranks only 11th in SHAP (0.0533), indicating the model relied more on categorical mix and raw debt.
cross_layer_validation.training_to_xai: Training diagnostics flagged class1 as most confused; PDP/ALE for Credit_Mix and Outstanding_Debt reveal non-linear boundaries that likely underlie class1’s porous boundary, aligning with the observed low recall and confidence for class1.
cross_layer_validation.fe_impact: Engineered EMI_Salary_Ratio appears at rank 11 in SHAP and 6 in PFI—much lower than expected—while Interest_Delay ranks 7th in SHAP (0.098) but low in PFI, suggesting these interaction features had limited marginal benefit.

tested_predictions.hypothesis: Removing Credit_Mix from the model will reduce macro_f1 by >5pp
tested_predictions.basis: Credit_Mix is #1 in both SHAP (0.4982) and PFI (0.1591)
tested_predictions.how_to_test: Retrain the model without Credit_Mix and measure change in macro_f1

tested_predictions.hypothesis: Permuting Month values will have minimal impact on macro_f1 (<1pp drop)
tested_predictions.basis: Month ranks #3 in SHAP (0.2199) but only #10 in PFI (0.0030)
tested_predictions.how_to_test: Shuffle Month in validation set and evaluate macro_f1

tested_predictions.hypothesis: Setting Outstanding_Debt to its median will shift predicted probabilities from class2 to class1 for ≥20% of cases
tested_predictions.basis: Outstanding_Debt shows a U-shaped effect on class2 in PDP, with mid-range debt shifting probability to class1
tested_predictions.how_to_test: Replace Outstanding_Debt by median for all instances and compare class probability distributions

supported_conjectures.hypothesis: Delay_from_due_date contributes mainly via interactions rather than main effects
supported_conjectures.basis: High grouped PFI rank (#3) vs lower SHAP rank (#6)
supported_conjectures.evidence_needed: 2-way SHAP interaction values with Outstanding_Debt and Credit_Mix

supported_conjectures.basis: Monotonic PDP but low PFI suggests interaction with Month drives its predictive power
supported_conjectures.evidence_needed: Conditional PDP of Num_Credit_Card split by Month categories

supported_conjectures.basis: Interest_Delay shap=0.098 but PFI=0.0025 and both parent features show non-monotonic PDP
supported_conjectures.evidence_needed: 2D ALE showing joint effect of Interest_Rate and Delay_from_due_date

exploratory_leads.hypothesis: Introducing Debt_Income_Ratio (Outstanding_Debt/Annual_Income) will exceed the importance of Outstanding_Debt alone
exploratory_leads.basis: Outstanding_Debt is high in both SHAP and PFI but debt burden ratio may capture additional income context
exploratory_leads.implication: Improved stratification of borrowers by debt burden

exploratory_leads.hypothesis: Binning Outstanding_Debt into quartiles will better capture its U-shaped effect, reducing prediction variance
exploratory_leads.basis: U-shaped PDP indicates non-linear segments of debt risk
exploratory_leads.implication: More stable class probability predictions across debt segments

exploratory_leads.hypothesis: Training separate models per Credit_Mix category will boost recall for class1 by >5pp
exploratory_leads.basis: Credit_Mix dominates global SHAP and defines distinct borrower segments
exploratory_leads.implication: Segmented modeling may reduce class1 boundary bleed

0.representative_profile: Row 17984: class 0 is easiest when Outstanding_Debt is very high (shap+0.5411) and Interest_Rate is high (shap+0.3569) with supportive Credit_Mix (shap+0.1742).
0.borderline_story: Row 17548: true 0 predicted 0 at only 0.3955 confidence because Delay_from_due_date (shap+0.5896) pushes strongly toward class 0 but moderate Amount_invested_monthly (shap−0.4351) and lower Outstanding_Debt (shap−0.2437) pull away, revealing a thin debt–investment boundary.
0.worst_misclassification_story: Row 12009: true 0 → predicted 1 with conf 0.879; high Credit_Mix (shap+0.5454) and high Interest_Rate (shap+0.4494) drive the score toward class 1, while its relatively low Outstanding_Debt (shap−0.2533) undermines the class 0 signal.

1.representative_profile: Row 18321: class 1 is easiest when Month is late in year (shap+0.9517), Credit_Mix is strong (shap+0.7922) and Outstanding_Debt is high (shap+0.5686).
1.borderline_story: Row 3191: true 1 predicted 1 at just 0.3372 confidence; low Credit_Mix (shap−0.6547) disfavors class 1 while small Outstanding_Debt (shap+0.1423) and modest Num_Bank_Accounts (shap+0.1008) only weakly support it, indicating Credit_Mix threshold splits class 1 from others.
1.worst_misclassification_story: Row 5976: true 1 → predicted 2 with conf 0.962; very high Credit_Mix (shap+0.9918) and many Credit_Cards (shap+0.6461) push to class 2, while Month slightly away (shap−0.1754) weakens class 1 evidence.

2.representative_profile: Row 4577: class 2 is easiest when Credit_Mix is at maximum (shap+0.9975) and Num_Credit_Card is high (shap+0.6201), with seasonal Month early in year (shap−0.1743).
2.borderline_story: Row 12245: true 2 predicted 2 at 0.3545 confidence; moderate Credit_Mix (shap+0.5275) pushes toward class 2 but low Num_Credit_Card (shap−0.3144) and few Bank_Accounts (shap−0.1120) pull back, highlighting a fragile card‐count boundary.
2.worst_misclassification_story: Row 17916: true 2 → predicted 1 with conf 0.8637; Credit_Mix (shap+0.5560) and elevated Outstanding_Debt (shap+0.3529) tilt toward class 1, while Month (shap−0.2240) fails to sustain class 2.

confusion_patterns.dominant_direction: The model over‐predicts class 1 under uncertainty: errors from class 0→1 (row 12009) and class 2→1 (row 17916) are high‐confidence.

decision_boundary_analysis.thinnest_boundary: The class 1 boundary is most fragile (row 3191 conf 0.3372) and hinges on mid-tier Credit_Mix values.
decision_boundary_analysis.boundary_candidate_for_engineering: One-hot encode Credit_Mix categories to break the ordinal assumption and sharpen the class 1 boundary.

tested_predictions.hypothesis: One-hot encoding Credit_Mix will reduce class 2→1 and class 0→1 misclassification rates by >10%.
tested_predictions.basis: Rows 12009, 17916 show high-confidence class 1 errors driven by Credit_Mix.
tested_predictions.how_to_test: Retrain model with one-hot Credit_Mix and compare class 1 misclassification rates on validation.

supported_conjectures.hypothesis: A mid-range Outstanding_Debt threshold creates bleed between class 0 and class 1.
supported_conjectures.basis: Borderline rows 17548 and 3191 show shap shifts around Outstanding_Debt.
supported_conjectures.evidence_needed: Analyze recall and precision by debt-bin to confirm a critical debt threshold.

exploratory_leads.hypothesis: Introducing Debt_Income_Ratio bins will capture non-linear risk segments seen in rows 5976 and 17916.
exploratory_leads.basis: Rows 5976 and 17916 show income context missing in high debt misclassifications.
exploratory_leads.implication: Better stratification of borrowers by debt burden may reduce high-confidence errors.

feature_engineering_hypothesis.interactions_rationale: Engineered four interaction features: EMI_Salary_Ratio (Total_EMI_per_month/Monthly_Inhand_Salary) because both are top MI features (0.5471, 0.5187) and uncorrelated; Invest_Salary_Ratio to capture savings rate; Debt_Income_Ratio (Outstanding_Debt/Annual_Income) as a domain debt burden indicator; Interest_Delay (Interest_Rate×Delay_from_due_date) to capture joint risk (MI parents=0.1705,0.1215).
feature_engineering_hypothesis.dropped_features_rationale: Dropped Credit_Utilization_Ratio since its mutual_information=0.0, contributing no predictive signal. No other high-correlation pairs were found.
feature_engineering_hypothesis.expected_impact: EMI_Salary_Ratio is expected to exceed MI of individual parents (>0.60), improving discrimination. Interest_Delay will capture non-linear risk segments for tree models. Log1p transforms on skewed features (e.g. Total_EMI_per_month skew=10.19→~1) will aid convergence and coefficient stability in linear models. Dual views optimize both model families.
eda_hypotheses_acted_on: exploratory_leads: missing indicator for Monthly_Inhand_Salary skipped (imputation removed missingness signal)

selection_justification: XGBoost achieved the highest macro_f1 score of 0.6902, showing better balance across classes, especially in handling class '1' with a good precision-recall balance. It also had the highest accuracy and weighted_f1, indicating overall robustness. The CV-test gap for XGBoost is minimal (0.6733 CV score vs. 0.6935 test accuracy), suggesting good generalization.
